summary: Matt Rocklin argues that while big collection frameworks like Spark and Dask collections are popular for parallel computing, many real-world workloads are too complex for their limited operation sets. He introduces dask.do (later renamed dask.delayed), a new interface that lets users write normal Python code with function calls that build dask graphs instead of executing immediately. This approach, inspired by joblib and implemented by Jim Crist, makes custom parallel workflows more accessible than manually constructing dask dictionaries. The post demonstrates the concept with a nested cross-validation example that achieves 4x speedup with minimal code changes.
key_points: Big collection frameworks (Spark, Dask arrays/dataframes) handle common cases well but force users to fall back to multiprocessing or perform 'mental gymnastics' for complex workloads
Manually constructing dask graphs is powerful but unintuitive and error-prone according to user feedback
The dask.do function wraps normal Python functions to build computation graphs lazily rather than executing immediately
The interface was inspired by Gael Varoquaux's experience with joblib and keeps parallel code looking similar to sequential code
Real-world example shows nested cross-validation parallelized with minimal changes, achieving 4x speedup
money_quotes: Parallel databases, Spark, and Dask collections all provide large distributed collections that handle parallel computation for you.
However, many workloads are too complex for these collections.
Perform mental gymnastics to fit their problem into Spark using a clever choice of keys. These cases often fail to achieve much speedup.
Feedback from users is that this is interesting and powerful but that programming directly in dictionaries is not intuitive, doesn't integrate well with IDEs, and is prone to error.
The explicit function calls here don't perform work directly; instead they build up a dask graph which we can then execute in parallel with our choice of scheduler.
The take-away is that it's somewhat involved but that the addition of parallelism is light.
key_insight: Custom parallel workflows shouldn't require users to abandon familiar Python patterns—wrapping functions to build lazy computation graphs bridges the gap between usability and flexibility.
